readingQuestions.prompt: 本文を読んで各設問に答えてください。

payload.title: The Hidden Network Beneath the Forest
paragraphs: When we walk through a forest, we tend to see the trees as separate individuals, each one quietly competing with its neighbours for light and water. Beneath our feet, however, lies a very different story. The soil is threaded with tiny fungal filaments that connect the roots of one tree to those of another, forming a vast underground web. Scientists sometimes call this system the wood wide web, because it links hundreds of trees into a single living network.

Through these fungal threads, trees can actually share resources. A tall tree whose leaves reach the sunlight may send sugars down to a smaller, shaded seedling that cannot yet make enough food of its own. When one tree is attacked by insects, chemical signals can travel along the network and warn its neighbours, giving them time to raise their own defences. In this way, a forest behaves less like a crowd of strangers and more like a community that depends on cooperation.

This discovery has changed how many biologists think about competition in nature. For a long time, the natural world was described mainly as a struggle in which only the strongest survive. The forest network suggests that survival can also depend on giving as well as taking. Of course, the fungi are not acting out of kindness; they receive sugars from the trees in return. Yet the result is a partnership in which both sides gain, reminding us that cooperation, not just competition, has shaped life on Earth.

questions.prompt: 本文の主旨として最も適切なものはどれですか。
options: Trees are linked by an underground fungal network that lets them share resources and warnings, showing that cooperation, not only competition, shapes nature.
Trees in a forest never interact in any way and each one survives completely on its own.
Fungi in the soil are harmful parasites that slowly destroy every tree they touch.
Scientists have proven that competition plays no role at all in how forests grow.

questions.after: down through the network to a smaller seedling growing in the shade.

questions.prompt: 木々がお互いに危険を知らせ合うことを示す語句はどれですか。

spans.text: each one quietly competing with its neighbours for light and water
chemical signals can travel along the network and warn its neighbours
the fungi are not acting out of kindness
the natural world was described mainly as a struggle

questions.sentence: In other words, both the trees and the fungi benefit from the exchange.

questions.prompt: 第3段落で筆者が最も伝えたいことはどれですか。
options: The forest network suggests that survival can depend on cooperation and giving, not only on competition, though the fungi still gain sugars in return.
The discovery proves that trees act purely out of kindness and expect nothing in return.
Biologists now believe that only the strongest trees can ever survive in a forest.
The fungal network has no effect on how scientists understand life in nature.

readingQuestions.prompt: 本文を読んで各設問に答えてください。

payload.title: The Return of Night Trains
paragraphs: A few decades ago, night trains were slowly disappearing across Europe. Cheap flights made it faster and often cheaper to cross the continent by air, and many railway companies quietly closed their sleeper services. For a while, it seemed that travelling overnight in a moving bed would become nothing more than a romantic memory from old films.

Recently, however, the night train has begun to make a surprising comeback. As more travellers worry about the pollution caused by flying, sleeping on a train has started to look attractive again. New routes now connect cities that are hundreds of kilometres apart, allowing passengers to fall asleep in one country and wake up in another. Instead of wasting an evening at an airport, travellers can enjoy dinner, sleep through the night, and arrive rested in the very centre of a city.

The revival is not without its problems. Running comfortable sleeper carriages is expensive, and tickets are not always cheaper than a plane seat. Even so, supporters believe that night trains offer something that flights cannot: a slower, calmer way to travel that is gentler on the planet. For many passengers, the chance to watch the countryside slide past the window before drifting off to sleep is reason enough to choose the train.

questions.prompt: 本文が最も伝えたいことはどれですか。
options: Night trains are returning because they offer a calmer, greener way to travel, even though they can be costly to run.
Night trains have completely disappeared and will never be used again anywhere in Europe.
Flying is now banned in every European country because of pollution.
Night trains are always far cheaper and faster than any possible flight.

questions.before: New routes now let passengers fall asleep in one country and

questions.prompt: なぜ夜行列車がかつて姿を消していったのかを説明している語句はどれですか。

spans.text: Cheap flights made it faster and often cheaper to cross the continent by air
sleeping on a train has started to look attractive again
travellers can enjoy dinner, sleep through the night, and arrive rested
a slower, calmer way to travel that is gentler on the planet

questions.prompt: 本文全体の要点はどれですか。
options: Concern about pollution is bringing night trains back as a relaxing, eco-friendly alternative to flying, despite their high running costs.
Night trains vanished forever because no one ever enjoyed travelling on them.
Air travel produces no pollution, so there is no reason to choose the train.
Sleeper trains are now the cheapest form of transport in every situation.
